options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat a genetic cardiomyopathy unless there is a separate infection.

options.text: Immediate appendectomy
options.explanation: Appendectomy is unrelated to cardiomyopathy management.

options.text: Radiation therapy to the heart
options.explanation: Radiation therapy is not a treatment for dilated cardiomyopathy and may harm the heart.

question: Which treatment is most appropriate for catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia?

options.text: Beta-blockers with exercise restriction
options.explanation: Beta-blockers and avoidance of strenuous exercise reduce catecholamine-triggered ventricular arrhythmias in CPVT.

options.text: Aspirin and statin therapy
options.explanation: Aspirin and statins treat atherosclerotic disease, not inherited ventricular tachyarrhythmias.

options.text: Thrombolysis for acute clot dissolution
options.explanation: Thrombolysis treats acute thrombotic occlusion, not catecholamine-induced arrhythmias.

options.text: Loop diuretics for volume overload
options.explanation: Loop diuretics relieve fluid overload but do not prevent CPVT-related ventricular tachycardia.

question: What is cardiomyopathy?

options.text: Disease of the heart muscle causing enlargement or stiffening
options.explanation: Cardiomyopathy refers to diseases of the heart muscle that can enlarge or stiffen it.

options.text: Inflammation of the heart lining
options.explanation: Inflammation of the heart lining is endocarditis or pericarditis, not cardiomyopathy.

options.text: Narrowing of the coronary arteries
options.explanation: Narrowing of the coronary arteries describes coronary artery disease, not heart muscle disease.

options.text: Infection of the heart valves
options.explanation: Infection of the heart valves is infective endocarditis, not cardiomyopathy.

question: What is a known risk factor for congenital heart defects such as atrial or ventricular septal defects?

options.text: Maternal smoking during pregnancy
options.explanation: Smoking during pregnancy has been linked to several congenital heart defects, including septal defects.

options.text: High maternal calcium intake
options.explanation: High maternal calcium intake is not a recognized cause of septal defects.

options.text: Fetal macrosomia
options.explanation: Fetal macrosomia is not a typical risk factor for congenital heart defects.

options.text: Maternal exercise during pregnancy
options.explanation: Maternal exercise during pregnancy is not known to cause congenital heart defects.

question: Which management approach is appropriate for familial thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection?

options.text: Genetic counseling, imaging surveillance, and surgical repair when indicated
options.explanation: Familial TAAD is managed with genetic counseling, surveillance, and surgery when the aorta reaches high-risk thresholds.

options.text: Antibiotic therapy alone
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat inherited aortic wall disease or prevent dissection.

options.text: Chemotherapy and radiation therapy
options.explanation: Chemotherapy and radiation are not treatments for thoracic aortic aneurysm or dissection.

options.text: Insulin and dietary restriction
options.explanation: Insulin and dietary restriction are unrelated to the management of familial TAAD.

question: What is the primary purpose of angioplasty in coronary artery disease?

options.text: To widen a narrowed artery and restore blood flow
options.explanation: Angioplasty uses a balloon to push plaque aside and reopen the narrowed artery, restoring blood flow.

options.text: To dissolve plaque with medication
options.explanation: Angioplasty does not dissolve plaque; it mechanically widens the vessel.

options.text: To replace the blocked artery with a graft
options.explanation: Bypass grafting replaces blood flow around a blockage, not angioplasty.

options.text: To remove the heart muscle damaged by infarction
options.explanation: Angioplasty does not remove damaged heart muscle after a heart attack.

question: Which statement best describes familial progressive cardiac conduction defect (PCCD)?

options.text: A conduction disorder that may progress to complete heart block
options.explanation: PCCD is a cardiac conduction disorder that can progressively worsen to complete heart block.

options.text: A valvular disease causing mitral regurgitation and syncope
options.explanation: Mitral regurgitation is a valve disorder, not a primary conduction defect.

options.text: An inflammatory cardiomyopathy caused by viral infection
options.explanation: PCCD is genetic and conduction-based, not an inflammatory viral cardiomyopathy.

options.text: A congenital cyanotic heart defect with right-to-left shunt
options.explanation: PCCD does not cause structural cyanotic shunt lesions.

question: What best describes postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS)?

options.text: Orthostatic intolerance with rapid heart rate on standing
options.explanation: POTS is characterized by orthostatic intolerance with a marked increase in heart rate upon standing.

options.text: Chest pain caused by coronary artery spasm
options.explanation: Coronary artery spasm causes angina, not posture-related tachycardia and orthostatic symptoms.

options.text: Progressive muscle weakness from neuromuscular junction failure
options.explanation: Neuromuscular junction failure causes fatigable weakness, not orthostatic intolerance.

options.text: Syncope due to fixed aortic valve obstruction
options.explanation: Aortic stenosis can cause syncope, but it is not defined by tachycardia on standing.

question: Which finding is most consistent with an innocent heart murmur?

options.text: No symptoms other than the murmur
options.explanation: Innocent heart murmurs are not caused by heart disease and usually have no symptoms besides the murmur itself.

options.text: Bluish lips and fingertips
options.explanation: Bluish lips and fingertips suggest abnormal circulation or heart disease, not an innocent murmur.

options.text: Shortness of breath with exertion
options.explanation: Exertional shortness of breath is a symptom of an abnormal murmur or underlying heart problem.

options.text: Poor feeding and failure to thrive
options.explanation: Poor feeding and failure to thrive in infants indicate a pathologic cause rather than an innocent murmur.

question: Which blood pressure reading is classified as pre-hypertension in an adult without diabetes or chronic kidney disease?

options.text: 118/76 mmHg
options.explanation: 118/76 mmHg is within the normal blood pressure range.

options.text: 128/70 mmHg
options.explanation: 128/70 mmHg falls in the pre-hypertension range because the systolic value is 120-139 mmHg.

options.text: 142/92 mmHg
options.explanation: 142/92 mmHg meets the threshold for high blood pressure, not pre-hypertension.

options.text: 110/60 mmHg
options.explanation: 110/60 mmHg is normal and below the pre-hypertension range.

question: What best describes carotid artery disease?

options.text: Narrowing of neck arteries from atherosclerotic plaque
options.explanation: Carotid artery disease is narrowing of the carotid arteries, usually from atherosclerotic plaque buildup.

options.text: Inflammation of carotid veins causing leg swelling
options.explanation: Carotid arteries are arteries, not veins, and this condition does not cause leg swelling.

options.text: Congenital absence of carotid blood vessels
options.explanation: The disease involves narrowing, not absence, of the carotid arteries.

options.text: Infection of the carotid artery by bacteria
options.explanation: The usual cause is atherosclerosis, not bacterial infection.

question: Which statement about CPR is most accurate?

options.text: It is an emergency procedure for cardiac arrest or stopped breathing.
options.explanation: CPR is used in emergencies when the heart has stopped or breathing has ceased to maintain circulation and oxygenation.

options.text: It is only useful after a stroke has fully resolved.
options.explanation: CPR is not limited to stroke recovery; it is for cardiac arrest or respiratory arrest.

options.text: It should never be started before medical help arrives.
options.explanation: CPR should be started immediately while waiting for emergency help, not delayed.

options.text: It replaces the need for emergency services.
options.explanation: CPR supports life temporarily but does not replace professional emergency medical care.

question: Which management approach is appropriate for a patient with syncope?

options.text: Check airway and breathing, then keep the patient lying down
options.explanation: Initial syncope management includes ensuring airway and breathing, then resting supine for recovery.

options.text: Immediately give oral glucose to all patients
options.explanation: Oral glucose is only indicated if hypoglycemia is suspected, not for all syncope cases.

options.text: Place the patient upright and encourage walking
options.explanation: Upright posture can worsen cerebral hypoperfusion and delay recovery.

options.text: Restrict fluids and keep the patient standing
options.explanation: Standing and fluid restriction can aggravate fainting rather than treat it.

question: Which condition is a pericardial disorder characterized by inflammation of the sac surrounding the heart?

options.text: Pericarditis
options.explanation: Pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardial sac surrounding the heart.

options.text: Myocarditis
options.explanation: Myocarditis is inflammation of the heart muscle, not the pericardium.

options.text: Endocarditis
options.explanation: Endocarditis affects the inner lining or valves of the heart, not the pericardium.

options.text: Cardiomyopathy
options.explanation: Cardiomyopathy is a disease of the heart muscle, not a pericardial disorder.

question: Which statement best describes heart diseases?

options.text: Only congenital defects affecting the heart valves
options.explanation: Heart disease is not limited to congenital valve defects; it includes many acquired conditions too.

options.text: A group of disorders including coronary artery disease, valve disease, and heart failure
options.explanation: Heart diseases include several conditions such as coronary artery disease, valve problems, heart failure, and congenital heart disease.

options.text: Infections of the heart muscle caused by bacteria
options.explanation: Bacterial infection of the heart muscle is only one rare cause, not the overall definition of heart disease.

options.text: Temporary chest pain from anxiety or stress
options.explanation: Anxiety-related chest pain is not a heart disease and does not describe the broad category.

question: Which set of findings characterizes Tetralogy of Fallot?

options.text: VSD, pulmonary stenosis, overriding aorta, right ventricular hypertrophy
options.explanation: Tetralogy of Fallot consists of four classic defects: VSD, pulmonary stenosis, overriding aorta, and right ventricular hypertrophy.

options.text: ASD, aortic regurgitation, left ventricular hypertrophy, mitral stenosis
options.explanation: These findings describe a different combination of structural and valvular abnormalities, not Tetralogy of Fallot.

options.text: Coarctation, PDA, bicuspid aortic valve, left atrial enlargement
options.explanation: This option lists lesions associated with other congenital heart diseases, not the classic TOF tetrad.

options.text: Tricuspid atresia, transposition, pulmonary atresia, left ventricular hypertrophy
options.explanation: These defects are not the defining features of Tetralogy of Fallot and do not form its classic tetrad.

question: Which measure helps prevent infective endocarditis in at-risk patients?

options.text: Maintain good oral hygiene and regular dental checkups
options.explanation: Good oral hygiene reduces gum infections that can seed bacteria into the bloodstream.

options.text: Take antibiotics before every routine dental cleaning
options.explanation: Antibiotics are not needed before every routine dental procedure for all at-risk patients.

options.text: Avoid all physical exercise
options.explanation: Physical exercise does not prevent infective endocarditis.

options.text: Use daily anticoagulation therapy
options.explanation: Anticoagulation does not prevent bacterial infection of the endocardium.

question: Which measure best helps prevent heart valve disease caused by rheumatic fever?

options.text: Promptly treat strep throat with prescribed antibiotics
options.explanation: Treating streptococcal infections promptly can prevent rheumatic fever, which can damage heart valves.

options.text: Avoid all physical activity to reduce valve stress
options.explanation: Avoiding exercise does not prevent rheumatic fever or strep-related valve damage.

options.text: Take cholesterol medicine only after valve damage occurs
options.explanation: Cholesterol medicine may help some valve disease risk, but it does not prevent rheumatic fever.

options.text: Use diuretics to prevent tonsil infection
options.explanation: Diuretics do not treat strep infections or prevent rheumatic fever.

question: Which of the following is a standard treatment approach for heart failure?

options.text: Lifestyle changes, medications, and specialized care
options.explanation: Heart failure is managed with lifestyle changes, medications, and specialized care in advanced stages.

options.text: Antibiotics alone
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat the underlying causes or symptoms of heart failure unless infection is present.

options.text: Surgical cure in all cases
options.explanation: There is no universal surgical cure for heart failure, and treatment depends on cause and severity.

options.text: No treatment is needed
options.explanation: Heart failure requires ongoing management to reduce symptoms and prevent worsening.

question: What is the primary cause of carotid artery disease?

options.text: Damage to the inner carotid artery lining leading to plaque buildup
options.explanation: Carotid artery disease begins with injury to the inner arterial lining, which promotes plaque formation and narrowing.

options.text: Infection of the carotid artery wall by bacteria
options.explanation: Bacterial infection is not the usual cause of carotid artery disease.

options.text: Congenital narrowing of the carotid arteries from birth
options.explanation: Congenital narrowing is uncommon and does not explain typical atherosclerotic carotid disease.

options.text: Autoimmune destruction of the carotid artery media
options.explanation: Autoimmune destruction of the media is not the main mechanism of carotid artery disease.

question: Which patient is at increased risk for heart failure?

options.text: A 68-year-old African-American man with prior myocardial infarction
options.explanation: Older age, African-American race, prior heart attack, and male sex all increase heart failure risk.

options.text: A 25-year-old woman with no medical history
options.explanation: A young woman without risk factors is not in a high-risk group for heart failure.

options.text: A 40-year-old athlete with low body weight
options.explanation: Low body weight and athletic conditioning do not increase heart failure risk.

options.text: A 30-year-old man with seasonal allergies
options.explanation: Seasonal allergies are not a recognized risk factor for heart failure.

question: Which treatment is most appropriate for progressive familial heart block?

options.text: Pacemaker implantation
options.explanation: Progressive familial heart block is managed with pacing to treat symptomatic conduction disease and prevent bradyarrhythmias.

options.text: Antibiotic therapy
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not correct inherited conduction system disease.

options.text: Coronary artery bypass grafting
options.explanation: Bypass surgery treats coronary artery disease, not familial heart block.

options.text: Thrombolytic therapy
options.explanation: Thrombolytics treat acute thrombosis, not conduction block.

question: Which statement best describes familial atrial fibrillation?

options.text: Inherited disorder causing irregular atrial electrical activity and arrhythmia
options.explanation: Familial atrial fibrillation is an inherited condition causing abnormal atrial electrical activity and an irregular heartbeat.

options.text: Autoimmune destruction of the atrioventricular node
options.explanation: Autoimmune AV node destruction is not the mechanism of familial atrial fibrillation.

options.text: Congenital narrowing of the aortic valve
options.explanation: Aortic valve narrowing causes outflow obstruction, not inherited atrial arrhythmia.

options.text: Infectious inflammation of the pericardium
options.explanation: Pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardium, not a familial rhythm disorder.

question: Which statement best describes heart disease in women?

options.text: It affects only older women and is rare before menopause.
options.explanation: Heart disease can occur in women of all ages, not only older women.

options.text: It is caused mainly by coronary artery narrowing and includes MVD and broken heart syndrome.
options.explanation: Coronary artery disease is the most common cause, and women are also more affected by microvascular disease and broken heart syndrome.

options.text: It is usually due to infection of the heart valves.
options.explanation: Valve infection is not the main description of heart disease in women.

options.text: It occurs only in men because women are protected by hormones.
options.explanation: Women are not protected from heart disease, and it is a major cause of death in women.

question: Which of the following is a common symptom of worsening cardiomyopathy?

options.text: Shortness of breath with exertion
options.explanation: Cardiomyopathy often causes heart failure symptoms such as shortness of breath, especially with physical exertion.

options.text: Painless jaundice
options.explanation: Painless jaundice is more suggestive of liver or biliary disease, not cardiomyopathy.

options.text: Productive cough with fever
options.explanation: Productive cough with fever suggests an infection such as pneumonia, not cardiomyopathy.

options.text: Severe joint swelling
options.explanation: Severe joint swelling is not a typical symptom of cardiomyopathy.

question: Who is at increased risk for hypotension?

options.text: Older adults, people on diuretics, and pregnant patients
options.explanation: Older adults, certain medications, and pregnancy are all recognized risk factors for hypotension.

options.text: Only children with asthma
options.explanation: Asthma is not a typical risk factor for hypotension, and risk is not limited to children.

options.text: Only athletes with high blood pressure
options.explanation: Athletes are not the only group at risk, and high blood pressure does not specifically cause hypotension.

options.text: Only people with diabetes
options.explanation: Diabetes can contribute in some cases, but it is not the only or defining risk group.

question: Which set of findings is most consistent with cardiogenic shock?

options.text: Confusion, rapid heartbeat, pale skin, weak pulse, and cool extremities
options.explanation: Cardiogenic shock causes poor organ perfusion, leading to confusion, tachycardia, pallor, weak pulse, and cool extremities.

options.text: Fever, productive cough, and pleuritic chest pain
options.explanation: These findings suggest an infectious or pulmonary process, not shock from poor cardiac output.

options.text: Hypertension, bounding pulse, and flushed skin
options.explanation: Hypertension and bounding pulses are not typical of shock, which usually causes low perfusion signs.

options.text: Abdominal pain, diarrhea, and jaundice
options.explanation: These symptoms are gastrointestinal or hepatic and do not match cardiogenic shock.

question: Which statement best describes cardiac rehabilitation?

options.text: A medically supervised program after heart disease to improve recovery and reduce future risk
options.explanation: Cardiac rehabilitation is a supervised program that helps recovery and lowers the risk of future heart problems.

options.text: An emergency procedure to restore blood flow during acute myocardial infarction
options.explanation: Emergency reperfusion is not cardiac rehabilitation; it is an acute treatment for myocardial infarction.

options.text: A surgical treatment to replace damaged coronary arteries with grafts
options.explanation: Coronary artery bypass grafting is a surgery, not a rehabilitation program.

options.text: A medication-only plan for controlling blood pressure and cholesterol
options.explanation: Cardiac rehab includes exercise, education, and counseling, not medications alone.

question: Which statement best describes the inheritance of sick sinus syndrome?

options.text: Most cases are sporadic, but some genetic forms are autosomal dominant or recessive.
options.explanation: Most sick sinus syndrome cases are sporadic, though HCN4 and SCN5A mutations can cause inherited forms.

options.text: It is always inherited as an autosomal dominant disorder.
options.explanation: Autosomal dominant inheritance occurs only in some HCN4-related cases, not all sick sinus syndrome.

options.text: It is always inherited as an autosomal recessive disorder.
options.explanation: Autosomal recessive inheritance occurs only in some SCN5A-related cases, not all sick sinus syndrome.

options.text: It is never inherited and has no genetic forms.
options.explanation: Some cases are genetic and inherited, so it is not true that sick sinus syndrome is never inherited.

question: What is cor triatriatum?

options.text: A congenital extra atrial chamber dividing one atrium into two parts
options.explanation: Cor triatriatum is a rare congenital defect with an extra chamber in the left or right atrium.

options.text: A congenital absence of the atrial septum causing a single atrium
options.explanation: This describes a common atrial septal defect, not an extra atrial chamber.

options.text: A ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunting
options.explanation: A ventricular septal defect involves the ventricles, not an additional atrial chamber.

options.text: An acquired enlargement of both atria from chronic hypertension
options.explanation: Atrial enlargement from hypertension is acquired, not a congenital structural anomaly.

question: What is the most common cause of mitral valve prolapse?

options.text: Acquired rheumatic fever damage
options.explanation: Rheumatic fever can damage valves, but it is not the usual cause of mitral valve prolapse.

options.text: Congenital connective tissue abnormality
options.explanation: Mitral valve prolapse is usually congenital and often associated with inherited connective tissue abnormalities.

options.text: Bacterial endocarditis
options.explanation: Endocarditis can affect valves, but it does not typically cause primary mitral valve prolapse.

options.text: Coronary artery disease
options.explanation: Coronary artery disease affects blood flow, not the structural valve abnormalities seen in mitral valve prolapse.

question: Which measure is recommended to help prevent heart disease?

options.text: Maintain a healthy weight and exercise regularly
options.explanation: Regular exercise and maintaining a healthy weight are key recommended strategies to reduce heart disease risk.

options.text: Avoid all dietary fats completely
options.explanation: Complete avoidance of all dietary fats is not a standard prevention recommendation for heart disease.

options.text: Take antibiotics daily to prevent plaque buildup
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent atherosclerosis or heart disease in otherwise healthy people.

options.text: Rest as much as possible and avoid physical activity
options.explanation: Physical inactivity increases cardiovascular risk rather than preventing heart disease.

question: Which treatment approach is used for chronic atrial and intestinal dysrhythmia?

options.text: Pacemaker therapy and nutritional support
options.explanation: Chronic atrial and intestinal dysrhythmia is managed with supportive care such as pacemakers and nutritional support.

options.text: Antibiotics and bronchodilators
options.explanation: Antibiotics and bronchodilators do not treat this cardiac and gastrointestinal dysrhythmia.

options.text: Insulin and dialysis
options.explanation: Insulin and dialysis are used for diabetes and kidney failure, not dysrhythmia.

options.text: Radiation and chemotherapy
options.explanation: Radiation and chemotherapy are cancer treatments and are not appropriate here.

question: Which patient is at increased risk for heart palpitations?

options.text: A highly stressed person with anxiety
options.explanation: Anxiety and high stress are common risk factors for palpitations.

options.text: A patient with seasonal allergies only
options.explanation: Seasonal allergies alone are not a typical risk factor for palpitations.

options.text: A person with nearsightedness
options.explanation: Nearsightedness does not increase the risk of palpitations.

options.text: A patient with mild eczema
options.explanation: Mild eczema is unrelated to the development of palpitations.

question: Which treatment is commonly used for postural tachycardia syndrome (POTS)?

options.text: Increase salt and fluid intake
options.explanation: Increasing salt and fluid intake helps expand blood volume and improve orthostatic symptoms in POTS.

options.text: High-dose loop diuretics
options.explanation: Loop diuretics reduce blood volume and would worsen orthostatic intolerance.

options.text: Chronic corticosteroids
options.explanation: Chronic corticosteroids are not standard therapy for POTS and do not address the main problem.

options.text: Immediate pacemaker implantation
options.explanation: Pacemaker implantation is not a routine treatment for POTS because the issue is autonomic regulation, not bradyarrhythmia.

question: What is a cause of long QT syndrome?

options.text: Certain medications and medical conditions
options.explanation: Long QT syndrome can be acquired from medications or conditions like vomiting, diarrhea, and thyroid disorders.

options.text: Only bacterial infections
options.explanation: Bacterial infections are not a typical cause of long QT syndrome.

options.text: Excess dietary protein intake
options.explanation: Dietary protein intake does not cause prolonged QT intervals.

options.text: Isolated vitamin C deficiency
options.explanation: Vitamin C deficiency is not a recognized cause of long QT syndrome.

question: Which patient is at highest risk for renal artery stenosis due to atherosclerosis?

options.text: A 60-year-old man with smoking and diabetes
options.explanation: Atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis is associated with classic cardiovascular risk factors like smoking and diabetes.

options.text: A 22-year-old woman with no medical history
options.explanation: Young healthy adults without risk factors are less likely to have atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis.

options.text: A child with seasonal allergies
options.explanation: Seasonal allergies are not a recognized risk factor for renal artery stenosis.

options.text: A 35-year-old athlete with low cholesterol
options.explanation: Athletic activity and low cholesterol do not increase the risk of atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis.

question: What best describes a heart transplantation?

options.text: Replacing a diseased heart with a healthy donor heart
options.explanation: A heart transplant replaces a damaged or diseased heart with a healthy heart from a deceased donor.

options.text: Repairing heart valves with a catheter procedure
options.explanation: Catheter-based valve repair treats valve disease but does not replace the entire heart.

options.text: Bypassing blocked coronary arteries with grafts
options.explanation: Coronary bypass surgery improves blood flow but does not involve transplanting a heart.

options.text: Removing fluid from the pericardial sac
options.explanation: Pericardial drainage treats fluid around the heart, not end-stage heart failure.

question: Which of the following is a possible cause of chest pain?

options.text: Only myocardial infarction
options.explanation: Chest pain is not caused only by myocardial infarction because many non-heart-attack conditions can produce it.

options.text: Panic attacks, heartburn, and pneumonia
options.explanation: Chest pain can result from cardiac, digestive, musculoskeletal, psychiatric, and lung causes such as panic attacks, heartburn, and pneumonia.

options.text: Only musculoskeletal strain
options.explanation: Musculoskeletal strain is only one possible cause, not the only cause of chest pain.

options.text: Only lung cancer
options.explanation: Lung cancer is not the only cause of chest pain, and many more common causes exist.

question: Which option best describes treatments used for pulmonary arterial hypertension?

options.text: Drug therapy, surgery, rehabilitation, and palliative care
options.explanation: Pulmonary arterial hypertension management may include drug therapy, surgery, rehabilitation, and palliative care.

options.text: Antibiotics alone
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat pulmonary arterial hypertension unless there is a separate infection.

options.text: Insulin and dietary restriction
options.explanation: Insulin and dietary restriction are treatments for diabetes, not pulmonary arterial hypertension.

options.text: Radiation therapy and chemotherapy
options.explanation: Radiation therapy and chemotherapy are cancer treatments, not standard therapy for pulmonary arterial hypertension.

question: Which finding is most characteristic of patent ductus arteriosus in an infant?

options.text: Continuous heart murmur with possible signs of volume overload
options.explanation: PDA may present with a heart murmur alone or with symptoms of pulmonary overcirculation and heart volume overload.

options.text: Sudden cyanosis after birth with absent femoral pulses
options.explanation: This describes coarctation of the aorta, not patent ductus arteriosus.

options.text: Harsh systolic murmur at the left upper sternal border only
options.explanation: A harsh systolic murmur alone is not the classic presentation of PDA.

options.text: Diastolic murmur with bounding pulse and clubbing
options.explanation: Clubbing and a diastolic murmur are not typical early symptoms of PDA.

question: Which statement best describes pulmonary vein stenosis?

options.text: Narrowing of veins carrying oxygenated blood from lungs to the heart
options.explanation: Pulmonary vein stenosis is narrowing of the veins that return oxygen-rich blood from the lungs to the heart.

options.text: Obstruction of pulmonary arteries carrying blood from heart to lungs
options.explanation: That describes pulmonary artery obstruction, not pulmonary vein stenosis.

options.text: Failure of the mitral valve to open during diastole
options.explanation: That describes mitral stenosis, a left-sided valvular disease.

options.text: Inflammation of bronchi causing airflow limitation
options.explanation: That describes bronchitis or asthma, not a vascular narrowing disorder.

question: What is syncope?

options.text: Temporary loss of consciousness from sudden reduced brain blood flow
options.explanation: Syncope is a brief loss of consciousness caused by decreased cerebral blood flow.

options.text: Persistent loss of consciousness from irreversible brain injury
options.explanation: Irreversible brain injury causes coma or permanent deficits, not syncope.

options.text: Seizure caused by abnormal electrical brain activity
options.explanation: Seizures are due to abnormal neuronal firing, not transient hypoperfusion.

options.text: Transient weakness due to low blood sugar
options.explanation: Hypoglycemia can cause altered mental status, but it is not the definition of syncope.

question: Which set of symptoms is most consistent with a heart attack?

options.text: Chest pressure with arm, jaw, or back pain; shortness of breath; nausea
options.explanation: Heart attacks commonly cause chest discomfort with radiation, shortness of breath, nausea, and sweating.

options.text: Sudden fever, productive cough, and wheezing
options.explanation: These symptoms suggest a respiratory infection or asthma, not typical myocardial infarction.

options.text: Severe headache, neck stiffness, and photophobia
options.explanation: These findings are more consistent with meningitis or another neurologic condition.

options.text: Abdominal bloating, diarrhea, and rash
options.explanation: These symptoms are more suggestive of gastrointestinal or allergic disease, not a heart attack.

question: What is the definitive treatment for symptomatic sick sinus syndrome?

options.text: Permanent pacemaker implantation
options.explanation: Symptomatic sick sinus syndrome is treated definitively with a permanent pacemaker to prevent bradycardia and pauses.

options.text: Beta-blocker therapy
options.explanation: Beta-blockers can worsen sinus node dysfunction and are not definitive treatment.

options.text: Catheter ablation
options.explanation: Catheter ablation treats tachyarrhythmias, not sinus node failure.

options.text: Thrombolytic therapy
options.explanation: Thrombolytic therapy is used for acute thrombosis, not sick sinus syndrome.

question: Which congenital heart defect is characterized by underdevelopment of the mitral valve, left ventricle, aortic valve, and aorta?

options.text: Hypoplastic left heart syndrome
options.explanation: HLHS is defined by incomplete development of the left-sided heart structures, including the mitral valve, left ventricle, aortic valve, and aorta.

options.text: Tetralogy of Fallot
options.explanation: Tetralogy of Fallot involves four different defects, not primary underdevelopment of the left heart.

options.text: Transposition of the great arteries
options.explanation: Transposition of the great arteries is a great vessel switching defect, not left-sided hypoplasia.

options.text: Coarctation of the aorta
options.explanation: Coarctation of the aorta is a narrowing of the aorta, not a syndrome of multiple underdeveloped left heart structures.

question: Which management approach is most appropriate for postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS)?

options.text: Increase salt and fluid intake, exercise, and use selected medications
options.explanation: POTS is managed with lifestyle measures to expand volume and improve circulation, plus medications in some patients.

options.text: Immediate pacemaker implantation for all patients
options.explanation: Pacemakers are not routine treatment for POTS and are reserved for specific conduction disorders.

options.text: Long-term high-dose corticosteroids as first-line therapy
options.explanation: Corticosteroids are not standard first-line therapy for POTS and do not address the usual mechanisms.

options.text: Strict bed rest and complete activity avoidance
options.explanation: Complete bed rest can worsen deconditioning and does not improve POTS symptoms long term.

question: Which management approach is most appropriate for retinal arterial macroaneurysm with supravalvular pulmonic stenosis?

options.text: Observation with retinal monitoring and cardiac evaluation
options.explanation: Management is typically supportive and condition-specific, including retinal surveillance and evaluation of associated cardiac disease.

options.text: Immediate chemotherapy
options.explanation: Chemotherapy has no role in treating retinal arterial macroaneurysm or pulmonic stenosis.

options.text: High-dose antibiotics
options.explanation: Antibiotics are not indicated unless there is an infectious cause, which this condition is not.

options.text: Emergency splenectomy
options.explanation: Splenectomy is unrelated to the diagnosis and does not treat either ocular or cardiac abnormalities.

question: Which of the following is an appropriate management approach for familial atrial fibrillation?

options.text: Genetic counseling and standard atrial fibrillation management
options.explanation: Familial atrial fibrillation is managed with standard arrhythmia care and may include genetic counseling.

options.text: Antibiotic therapy alone
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat atrial fibrillation, which is a cardiac rhythm disorder.

options.text: Insulin therapy
options.explanation: Insulin treats diabetes, not familial atrial fibrillation.

options.text: Radiation therapy
options.explanation: Radiation therapy is not used to manage atrial fibrillation.

question: Which of the following is a common symptom of pulmonary hypertension?

options.text: Shortness of breath with routine activity
options.explanation: Pulmonary hypertension commonly causes exertional dyspnea, such as shortness of breath during routine activity.

options.text: Productive cough with fever
options.explanation: Fever and productive cough suggest infection, not a typical primary symptom of pulmonary hypertension.

options.text: Severe left lower quadrant pain
options.explanation: Left lower quadrant pain is not a characteristic symptom of pulmonary hypertension.

options.text: Jaundice and dark urine
options.explanation: Jaundice and dark urine are more consistent with hepatobiliary disease than pulmonary hypertension.

question: What best describes transposition of the great arteries?

options.text: Reversal of the aorta and pulmonary artery connections
options.explanation: TGA is a congenital defect where the aorta and pulmonary artery are switched, creating parallel circulations.

options.text: Narrowing of the aortic valve causing left ventricular outflow obstruction
options.explanation: This describes aortic stenosis, not transposition of the great arteries.

options.text: Failure of the atrial septum to close after birth
options.explanation: This describes an atrial septal defect, not TGA.

options.text: Persistent fetal connection between aorta and pulmonary artery
options.explanation: This describes a patent ductus arteriosus, not TGA.

question: Who is at increased risk for patent ductus arteriosus (PDA)?

options.text: Premature infants
options.explanation: PDA occurs more often in premature infants than in full-term infants.

options.text: Older children with asthma
options.explanation: Asthma in older children is not a recognized risk factor for PDA.

options.text: Term infants of diabetic mothers
options.explanation: Maternal diabetes is not a classic risk factor for PDA.

options.text: Male infants with no genetic conditions
options.explanation: PDA is more common in girls, not male infants.

question: Which treatment approach is recommended for diabetes-related heart disease and stroke prevention?

options.text: Meal planning, physical activity, medications, aspirin, and possibly surgery
options.explanation: Heart disease treatment includes diet, exercise, medications, aspirin, and sometimes procedures.

options.text: Antibiotics and bed rest only
options.explanation: Antibiotics and bed rest do not treat cardiovascular disease or diabetes complications.

options.text: Insulin alone without lifestyle changes
options.explanation: Insulin alone is insufficient because lifestyle changes and other medications are often needed.

options.text: Radiation therapy and chemotherapy
options.explanation: Radiation therapy and chemotherapy are not standard treatments for diabetes, heart disease, or stroke.

question: What is the most common cause of renal artery stenosis?

options.text: Atherosclerotic plaque buildup in the renal arteries
options.explanation: Atherosclerosis causes about 90% of renal artery stenosis by narrowing the renal arteries with plaque.

options.text: Fibromuscular dysplasia of the renal artery wall
options.explanation: Fibromuscular dysplasia is a less common cause of renal artery stenosis.

options.text: Renal artery vasculitis
options.explanation: Vasculitis can affect renal vessels but is not the usual cause of renal artery stenosis.

options.text: Congenital renal artery hypoplasia
options.explanation: Congenital hypoplasia is rare and not the typical cause of renal artery stenosis.

question: What is the appropriate treatment for dextrocardia with situs inversus?

options.text: No treatment unless associated defects are present
options.explanation: Treatment depends on associated heart or organ abnormalities; isolated dextrocardia with situs inversus often needs no specific therapy.

options.text: Immediate heart transplantation for all patients
options.explanation: Heart transplantation is not routinely indicated and is reserved for severe, specific cardiac disease.

options.text: Anticoagulation to prevent embolic stroke
options.explanation: Anticoagulation is not a standard treatment for dextrocardia with situs inversus.

options.text: Long-term corticosteroids for all cases
options.explanation: Corticosteroids are not used as routine treatment unless another inflammatory condition is present.

question: Who is at increased risk for broken heart syndrome?

options.text: Postmenopausal women in their 60s and 70s
options.explanation: Broken heart syndrome occurs more often in postmenopausal women, especially in their 60s and 70s.

options.text: Young men with hypertension and diabetes
options.explanation: Traditional coronary risk factors like hypertension and diabetes are not the main risk profile for this condition.

options.text: Children with congenital heart disease
options.explanation: Congenital heart disease is not a typical risk factor for broken heart syndrome.

options.text: Athletes with high resting heart rates
options.explanation: A high resting heart rate in athletes is not associated with increased risk of broken heart syndrome.

question: Which lifestyle choice is recommended to help reduce the risk of heart disease, cancer, and stroke?

options.text: Maintain a healthy weight and be physically active
options.explanation: Maintaining a healthy weight and exercising regularly are key healthy living steps that lower disease risk.

options.text: Increase saturated fat intake
options.explanation: Increasing saturated fat intake raises cardiovascular risk rather than reducing it.

options.text: Smoke occasionally to reduce stress
options.explanation: Smoking increases the risk of many serious diseases and is not a healthy living recommendation.

options.text: Avoid all screening tests
options.explanation: Screening tests help detect disease early and are recommended as part of healthy living.

question: Which description best defines congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries?

options.text: Aortic and pulmonary arteries arise from the correct ventricles
options.explanation: In this condition the great arteries do not arise from the correct ventricles.

options.text: Ventricles and their valves are switched, with associated defects common
options.explanation: This defect features atrioventricular and ventriculoarterial discordance, so the ventricles and valves are switched.

options.text: Only the atria are reversed, causing cyanosis at birth
options.explanation: Isolated atrial reversal does not describe congenitally corrected transposition.

options.text: The aorta is narrowed after the left ventricle
options.explanation: Aortic narrowing describes coarctation, not transposition.

question: Who is at increased risk for coronary microvascular disease?

options.text: Women with low estrogen levels
options.explanation: Low estrogen levels in women, especially before menopause, increase the risk of coronary microvascular disease.

options.text: Only elderly men with hypertension
options.explanation: Coronary microvascular disease affects both sexes and is not limited to elderly men with hypertension.

options.text: Children with congenital heart disease
options.explanation: Congenital heart disease in children is not a typical risk factor for coronary microvascular disease.

options.text: Athletes with high HDL levels
options.explanation: High HDL levels are generally protective rather than a risk factor for coronary microvascular disease.

question: Which approach best lowers the risk of heart disease and stroke in a person with diabetes?

options.text: Control A1C, blood pressure, and cholesterol
options.explanation: Controlling the ABCs of diabetes—A1C, blood pressure, and cholesterol—reduces cardiovascular risk.

options.text: Avoid all carbohydrates completely
options.explanation: Eliminating all carbohydrates is not the recommended strategy for reducing heart disease or stroke risk.

options.text: Take antibiotics regularly
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent diabetes-related heart disease or stroke.

options.text: Delay care until symptoms resolve
options.explanation: Warning signs of heart attack or stroke require immediate medical care, not delay.

question: Which statement best describes Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome?

options.text: Congenital accessory pathway causing tachyarrhythmias
options.explanation: WPW is a congenital conduction abnormality with an accessory pathway that predisposes to tachyarrhythmias.

options.text: Acquired valve disease causing bradycardia
options.explanation: Valve disease does not define WPW and typically does not cause the characteristic pre-excitation pattern.

options.text: Autoimmune myocarditis causing heart block
options.explanation: Myocarditis can cause arrhythmias, but it is not the congenital accessory pathway disorder of WPW.

options.text: Coronary artery spasm causing angina
options.explanation: Coronary spasm causes ischemic chest pain, not the electrical conduction abnormality seen in WPW.

question: Which statement best describes angina in relation to a heart attack?

options.text: A recurring chest discomfort caused by reduced blood flow to the heart
options.explanation: Angina is recurrent chest pain or discomfort from insufficient blood flow to the heart muscle.

options.text: A complete blockage of a coronary artery causing heart muscle death
options.explanation: A complete coronary blockage with heart muscle death describes a heart attack, not angina.

options.text: A type of heart valve infection causing chest pain
options.explanation: Heart valve infection is endocarditis and does not define angina.

options.text: A lung condition that causes shortness of breath and wheezing
options.explanation: A lung condition with wheezing is not the cause of angina.

question: Which set of symptoms is most consistent with second- or third-degree heart block?

options.text: Fainting, dizziness, fatigue, shortness of breath, and chest pain
options.explanation: Second- and third-degree heart block can cause syncope, dizziness, fatigue, dyspnea, and chest pain.

options.text: Polyuria, fever, rash, and abdominal pain
options.explanation: These symptoms are not typical features of heart block and suggest other systemic illnesses.

options.text: Headache, tremor, diarrhea, and weight loss
options.explanation: This cluster is more suggestive of thyroid or metabolic disease, not heart block.

options.text: Cough, wheezing, hemoptysis, and pleuritic pain
options.explanation: These are respiratory symptoms and do not match the usual presentation of heart block.

question: Which symptom is commonly associated with arrhythmia?

options.text: Palpitations and irregular heartbeat
options.explanation: Arrhythmias commonly cause palpitations and an irregular heartbeat.

options.text: Persistent high fever
options.explanation: High fever is not a typical symptom of arrhythmia.

options.text: Jaundice and dark urine
options.explanation: Jaundice and dark urine suggest liver disease, not arrhythmia.

options.text: Joint swelling and rash
options.explanation: Joint swelling and rash are not characteristic symptoms of arrhythmia.

question: Which set of symptoms is most typical of heart failure?

options.text: Shortness of breath, fatigue, and peripheral swelling
options.explanation: Heart failure commonly causes dyspnea, fatigue, and fluid-related swelling in the legs, ankles, feet, or abdomen.

options.text: Chest pain, fever, and productive cough
options.explanation: These symptoms suggest infection or ischemic heart disease, not the typical heart failure symptom pattern.

options.text: Abdominal pain, jaundice, and diarrhea
options.explanation: These are gastrointestinal or liver-related symptoms and are not characteristic of heart failure.

options.text: Headache, rash, and joint stiffness
options.explanation: These symptoms are more consistent with neurologic, dermatologic, or rheumatologic conditions than heart failure.

question: What is the effect of atrial fibrillation on stroke risk, and how does anticoagulation change that risk?

options.text: AF lowers stroke risk, and anticoagulation has little benefit
options.explanation: AF does not lower stroke risk, and anticoagulation provides major stroke prevention benefit.

options.text: AF increases stroke risk 4 to 6 times, and anticoagulation cuts it by about half to two-thirds
options.explanation: Atrial fibrillation raises stroke risk substantially, and warfarin or newer anticoagulants reduce that risk by about 50% to 67%.

options.text: AF only causes stroke in younger adults, and anticoagulation is ineffective
options.explanation: AF increases stroke risk across ages, especially in older adults, and anticoagulation is effective.

options.text: AF causes stroke only after valve surgery, and aspirin is superior to warfarin
options.explanation: AF can cause stroke without valve surgery, and anticoagulation is more effective than aspirin for prevention.

question: Which lifestyle change most effectively lowers blood pressure in overweight patients with hypertension?

options.text: Lose 7 to 10 percent of body weight over six months
options.explanation: Weight loss of 7 to 10 percent over six months can significantly reduce blood pressure, especially in overweight patients with hypertension.

options.text: Increase dietary sodium intake
options.explanation: Increasing sodium intake raises blood pressure rather than lowering it.

options.text: Avoid all physical activity
options.explanation: Avoiding physical activity worsens cardiovascular risk and does not lower blood pressure.

options.text: Gain 10 pounds to improve vascular tone
options.explanation: Weight gain increases blood pressure and is not a treatment for hypertension.

question: Which of the following is an appropriate treatment approach for heterotaxy syndrome?

options.text: Surgical correction of associated congenital heart defects
options.explanation: Heterotaxy syndrome is managed by treating associated anomalies, especially congenital heart defects, often with surgery.

options.text: Antibiotics alone as definitive therapy
options.explanation: Antibiotics may treat infections but do not correct the underlying structural abnormalities of heterotaxy syndrome.

options.text: Insulin therapy for metabolic control
options.explanation: Insulin is unrelated to the congenital structural defects seen in heterotaxy syndrome.

options.text: Radiation therapy to prevent organ malposition
options.explanation: Radiation therapy has no role in correcting organ laterality defects or associated cardiac malformations.

question: Which symptom pattern is most characteristic of Prinzmetal's variant angina?

options.text: Chest pain at rest, often midnight to 8 AM, relieved by nitroglycerin
options.explanation: Prinzmetal's variant angina typically causes rest pain with a circadian pattern and prompt relief with nitroglycerin.

options.text: Exertional chest pain relieved by rest and worsened by activity
options.explanation: This describes stable angina, which is triggered by exertion and relieved by rest.

options.text: Sharp pleuritic pain worsened by inspiration and coughing
options.explanation: This pattern is more consistent with pleurisy or pulmonary causes, not coronary vasospasm.

options.text: Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back
options.explanation: This is classic for aortic dissection, not variant angina.

question: What is Ebstein's anomaly?

options.text: A congenital tricuspid valve malformation causing right atrial regurgitation
options.explanation: Ebstein's anomaly is an abnormal tricuspid valve that leaks backward into the right atrium.

options.text: A congenital mitral valve stenosis causing left atrial enlargement
options.explanation: Mitral stenosis affects the left heart and does not describe Ebstein's anomaly.

options.text: A ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunting
options.explanation: A ventricular septal defect is a septal opening, not a tricuspid valve malformation.

options.text: An aortic valve defect causing systemic outflow obstruction
options.explanation: Aortic valve obstruction involves the left ventricular outflow tract, not the tricuspid valve.

question: What is high blood cholesterol primarily referring to?

options.text: Elevated LDL cholesterol in the blood
options.explanation: High blood cholesterol commonly refers to elevated LDL, which can form plaque in arteries.

options.text: Low HDL cholesterol only
options.explanation: Low HDL is a risk factor, but it is not what high blood cholesterol specifically means here.

options.text: High blood glucose levels
options.explanation: High blood glucose describes diabetes, not cholesterol.

options.text: Excess triglycerides in the liver
options.explanation: Triglycerides are a different lipid type and do not define high blood cholesterol.

question: Which description best defines hypoplastic right heart syndrome?

options.text: Underdevelopment of right-sided heart structures causing cyanosis
options.explanation: Hypoplastic right heart syndrome is a congenital underdevelopment of right-sided cardiac structures that leads to low oxygen levels and cyanosis.

options.text: Underdevelopment of left-sided heart structures causing systemic hypoperfusion
options.explanation: This describes hypoplastic left heart syndrome, not right heart syndrome.

options.text: Obstruction of the aortic valve causing left ventricular hypertrophy
options.explanation: Aortic valve obstruction causes aortic stenosis, not hypoplastic right heart syndrome.

options.text: Failure of the ductus arteriosus to close after birth
options.explanation: Persistent ductus arteriosus is a separate congenital defect and does not define this syndrome.

question: How common is familial restrictive cardiomyopathy?

options.text: It affects less than 5% of all cardiomyopathy cases
options.explanation: Less than 5% refers to restrictive cardiomyopathy overall, not specifically familial cases.

options.text: Its prevalence is unknown
options.explanation: The proportion of restrictive cardiomyopathy that runs in families is not known.

options.text: It affects about 25% of all cardiomyopathy cases
options.explanation: There is no evidence that familial restrictive cardiomyopathy affects 25% of cases.

options.text: It is the most common cardiomyopathy type
options.explanation: Restrictive cardiomyopathy is the least common cardiomyopathy type, not the most common.

question: What is the underlying cause of Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome?

options.text: An accessory atrioventricular conduction pathway present at birth
options.explanation: WPW is caused by an extra accessory pathway that is congenital and allows abnormal rapid conduction.

options.text: Autoimmune destruction of the sinoatrial node
options.explanation: Autoimmune SA node destruction causes sinus node dysfunction, not an accessory pathway.

options.text: Acquired fibrosis of the His-Purkinje system
options.explanation: Fibrosis of the His-Purkinje system causes conduction block, not preexcitation.

options.text: Viral myocarditis causing atrial enlargement
options.explanation: Viral myocarditis may affect heart function, but it does not cause WPW's congenital bypass tract.

question: Approximately how many American adults have elevated blood cholesterol levels?

options.text: More than 34 million
options.explanation: More than 34 million American adults have elevated blood cholesterol levels.

options.text: About 3.4 million
options.explanation: This is far below the reported number of affected adults.

options.text: About 340,000
options.explanation: This is much lower than the prevalence described.

options.text: About 34,000
options.explanation: This is orders of magnitude too low to match the data.

question: Which treatment is most appropriate for Jervell and Lange-Nielsen syndrome?

options.text: Beta-blockers with consideration of ICD placement
options.explanation: Beta-blockers and implantable cardioverter-defibrillator therapy are key management options for this congenital long-QT syndrome.

options.text: High-dose corticosteroids
options.explanation: Corticosteroids do not treat the cardiac ion-channel defect causing Jervell and Lange-Nielsen syndrome.

options.text: Antibiotic prophylaxis
options.explanation: Antibiotic prophylaxis is used for selected infections, not inherited arrhythmia syndromes.

options.text: Thyroid hormone replacement
options.explanation: Thyroid hormone replacement is unrelated to the underlying channelopathy and hearing loss.

question: Which description best defines Prinzmetal's variant angina?

options.text: Exertional chest pain relieved by rest and nitroglycerin
options.explanation: That describes typical stable angina, which is triggered by exertion and relieved by rest.

options.text: Recurrent chest pain at rest due to coronary vasospasm
options.explanation: Prinzmetal's variant angina causes recurrent rest pain from transient coronary vasospasm and is an unstable angina pattern.

options.text: Chest pain from aortic dissection with tearing radiation
options.explanation: Aortic dissection causes sudden tearing pain, not episodic vasospastic angina.

options.text: Stable chest pain caused by fixed atherosclerotic narrowing
options.explanation: Stable angina is predictable and exertional, unlike Prinzmetal's unpredictable rest pain.

question: Which lifestyle change is most helpful for preventing erectile dysfunction?

options.text: Eating a healthy diet, quitting smoking, and exercising regularly
options.explanation: Healthy diet, smoking cessation, and regular physical activity can reduce common causes of erectile dysfunction.

options.text: Taking daily antibiotics to improve blood flow
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent erectile dysfunction and are not used to improve penile blood flow.

options.text: Avoiding all physical activity to conserve energy
options.explanation: Avoiding physical activity worsens circulation rather than preventing erectile dysfunction.

options.text: Using testosterone supplements without medical advice
options.explanation: Testosterone should not be taken without evaluation and is not a general prevention strategy.

question: Which imaging study is typically preferred to confirm dextrocardia with situs inversus?

options.text: Chest x-ray
options.explanation: A chest x-ray may suggest the diagnosis but is not the preferred confirmatory test.

options.text: Computed tomography scan
options.explanation: CT scanning is typically the preferred examination to confirm dextrocardia with situs inversus.

options.text: Electrocardiogram
options.explanation: An electrocardiogram can show clues but cannot confirm the anatomic reversal.

options.text: Echocardiography only
options.explanation: Echocardiography may help assess cardiac structure but is not the standard confirmatory study.

question: Which management approach is appropriate for Timothy syndrome?

options.text: Diagnostic testing, drug therapy, surgery, and genetic counseling
options.explanation: Timothy syndrome management includes diagnostic evaluation, medications, surgery/rehabilitation, and genetic counseling.

options.text: Antibiotic therapy and bronchodilators only
options.explanation: Antibiotics and bronchodilators do not address the cardiac and congenital features of Timothy syndrome.

options.text: Chemotherapy and radiation therapy
options.explanation: Chemotherapy and radiation are not treatments for this genetic channelopathy.

options.text: Insulin therapy and dialysis
options.explanation: Insulin and dialysis are unrelated to the management of Timothy syndrome.

question: Which blood pressure pattern is considered normal in adults?

options.text: Systolic below 120 and diastolic below 80 mmHg
options.explanation: Adult normal blood pressure is defined as systolic below 120 mmHg and diastolic below 80 mmHg.

options.text: Systolic below 140 and diastolic below 90 mmHg
options.explanation: 140/90 mmHg is a common hypertension threshold, not normal adult blood pressure.

options.text: Systolic above 120 and diastolic above 80 mmHg
options.explanation: Values above 120/80 mmHg are not the definition of normal blood pressure.

options.text: Any blood pressure that changes with activity
options.explanation: Blood pressure can change with activity, but normality depends on the resting baseline range.

question: What are blood pressure medicines used for?

options.text: To lower blood pressure when lifestyle changes are not enough
options.explanation: They are used to lower blood pressure when lifestyle measures alone do not control hypertension.

options.text: To cure hypertension permanently
options.explanation: They control hypertension but do not permanently cure it.

options.text: To treat low blood pressure episodes
options.explanation: These medicines are intended for high blood pressure, not hypotension.

options.text: To replace the need for diet and exercise
options.explanation: Lifestyle changes remain important and medicines do not replace them.

question: What is dextrocardia?

options.text: Heart located on the right side of the chest, usually congenital
options.explanation: Dextrocardia is a congenital condition in which the heart lies on the right side of the chest.

options.text: Heart enlarged due to left ventricular hypertrophy
options.explanation: Left ventricular hypertrophy is thickening of the left ventricle, not right-sided heart position.

options.text: Heart displaced downward into the abdomen
options.explanation: A heart in the abdomen describes ectopia cordis or diaphragmatic displacement, not dextrocardia.

options.text: Heart rhythm originating from the right atrium
options.explanation: A rhythm from the right atrium refers to an arrhythmia, not abnormal heart location.

question: Which treatment is most appropriate for progressive cardiomyopathy in Danon disease?

options.text: High-dose corticosteroids
options.explanation: Corticosteroids do not treat the underlying cardiomyopathy in Danon disease.

options.text: Early heart transplantation or ICD placement
options.explanation: Danon disease can cause rapidly progressive cardiomyopathy, so early transplantation or an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator may be needed.

options.text: Antibiotic prophylaxis
options.explanation: Antibiotic prophylaxis is not a treatment for Danon-related heart failure.

options.text: Dietary iron restriction
options.explanation: Iron restriction has no role in managing Danon disease cardiomyopathy.

question: Which management approach is appropriate for Romano-Ward syndrome?

options.text: Beta-blockers and avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs
options.explanation: Romano-Ward syndrome is a long QT syndrome treated with beta-blockers and trigger avoidance.

options.text: High-dose antibiotics and anticoagulation
options.explanation: Antibiotics and anticoagulation do not treat inherited long QT syndrome.

options.text: Insulin therapy and dietary sodium restriction
options.explanation: Insulin and sodium restriction are unrelated to cardiac repolarization disorders.

options.text: Chemotherapy and radiation therapy
options.explanation: Chemotherapy and radiation are not used for this inherited arrhythmia syndrome.

question: Which statement best describes the inheritance of Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome?

options.text: Most cases are sporadic; a small familial form is autosomal dominant.
options.explanation: Most Wolff-Parkinson-White cases are sporadic, while the familial form is usually autosomal dominant.

options.text: It is always inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
options.explanation: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome is not typically inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.

options.text: It is always caused by a new mutation with no familial cases.
options.explanation: Familial cases do occur, so it is not always due to a new mutation.

options.text: It is exclusively inherited through the maternal line.
options.explanation: There is no exclusive maternal inheritance pattern for Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome.

question: Which management approach is recommended for catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT)?

options.text: No treatment is needed if the patient is asymptomatic
options.explanation: Asymptomatic CPVT can still carry risk, so observation alone is not sufficient.

options.text: All clinically diagnosed patients should receive treatment
options.explanation: All clinically diagnosed CPVT patients are recommended to receive treatment, even if asymptomatic.

options.text: Only patients with syncope require treatment
options.explanation: Treatment is not limited to patients with syncope because asymptomatic patients may still benefit.
